distractors.D: Comes from misreading the degree value as \(60^\circ\).

diagram.alt: Figure showing the angle x to be expressed in radians

questions.stem: Given that \(y = \sqrt{5 - x}\), then \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) is
questions.explanation: Write \(y = (5-x)^{1/2}\). Chain rule: \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \tfrac{1}{2}(5-x)^{-1/2}\cdot(-1) = \boldsymbol{-\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{5-x}}}\).

distractors.A: Forgets the \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) from the power rule.
distractors.B: Forgets both the \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) and the negative from the inner derivative \(-1\).
distractors.C: Forgets the inner derivative \(-1\) — drops the negative sign.

questions.stem: At the \(10\,\text{m}\) stopping point on a runway, an airplane is stationary before take-off. If the plane travels \(120\,\text{m}\) from this point in \(4\) seconds, what is its speed at the point of take off?
questions.explanation: Treating the motion as having an average speed equal to total distance over time gives \(\dfrac{120}{4} = \boldsymbol{30\,\text{m/s}}\). Note: a strict constant-acceleration model with \(u=0\), \(s=120\), \(t=4\) would give \(v=60\,\text{m/s}\) (since \(v = 2s/t\) for \(u=0\)); the published key uses the simpler average-speed reading.

distractors.A: Adds \(32\) by misusing \(s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2\).
distractors.B: Uses \(s/t\) but with the wrong distance \((108\,\text{m})\).
distractors.D: Uses \(v^2 = 2as\) with an incorrect acceleration estimate.

questions.stem: The curve \(C\) is given by the equation \(y = 2x^3 - 3x^2 - 12x + 6\). The values of \(x\) at which stationary points occur are
questions.explanation: Differentiate: \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 6x^2 - 6x - 12 = 6(x^2 - x - 2) = 6(x-2)(x+1)\). Setting this to zero gives \(x = 2\) or \(x = -1\).

distractors.A: Sign slip on factorisation — gets \((x-1)(x-2)\).
distractors.C: Both signs flipped on the factorisation.
distractors.D: Treats the cubic's factor pair both as negative.

questions.stem: Given an expression for the acceleration of a moving object, the velocity of the object can be found by
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the derivative of velocity: \(a = \dfrac{dv}{dt}\). Reversing this requires the inverse operation: integration, so \(v = \int a\,dt\).

distractors.A: Squaring is unrelated to the calculus relationship.
distractors.C: Differentiating velocity gives acceleration — this is the wrong direction.
distractors.D: Square rooting confuses with the SUVAT formula \(v = \sqrt{u^2 + 2as}\), which is a special case.

questions.stem: If \(X = \displaystyle\int_a^b f(x)\,dx\) and \(a < c < b\), then \(X =\)
questions.explanation: The additivity property of definite integrals: \(\int_a^b = \int_a^c + \int_c^b\) when \(a < c < b\). Splitting at any interior point gives equal area pieces that add to the whole.

distractors.A: Uses 0 as a lower limit, ignoring \(a\) entirely.
distractors.B: Reverses the limits on one piece, which would negate the integral.
distractors.D: Over-engineers the splitting with redundant zero-based pieces.

questions.stem: If \(c\) is a constant, then \(\displaystyle\int (2x-5)^3\,dx =\)
questions.explanation: Use the reverse chain rule: \(\int (ax+b)^n\,dx = \dfrac{(ax+b)^{n+1}}{a(n+1)} + c\). Here \(a=2\), \(n=3\), so the answer is \(\dfrac{(2x-5)^4}{2 \cdot 4} = \dfrac{(2x-5)^4}{8}\).

distractors.A: Forgets to divide by the inner derivative \(a = 2\).
distractors.B: Differentiates instead of integrating — drops the power by one.
distractors.D: Mishandles both the power and the inner derivative.

questions.stem: If \(c\) is a constant, then \(\displaystyle\int (\sin x + 2\cos x)\,dx =\)
questions.explanation: \(\int \sin x\,dx = -\cos x\) and \(\int \cos x\,dx = \sin x\), so \(\int (\sin x + 2\cos x)\,dx = \boldsymbol{-\cos x + 2\sin x + c}\) (option A).

distractors.B: Uses the wrong sign on \(\int \sin x\,dx\) — forgets the negative.
distractors.C: Both terms have wrong sign — confuses sin and cos integration.
distractors.D: Wrong sign on \(\cos x\) — confused with differentiation.

questions.stem: There are 108 cards in a deck of UNO playing cards. There are four "Wild", four "Wild Draw Four" and 25 each of four colours (red, yellow, green, blue). If a player needs to select 1 card, what is the probability of NOT drawing a "Wild Draw Four"?
questions.explanation: P(Wild Draw Four) \(= \dfrac{4}{108} = \dfrac{1}{27}\). So P(NOT Wild Draw Four) \(= 1 - \dfrac{1}{27} = \boldsymbol{\dfrac{26}{27}}\).

distractors.B: Confuses with the count of one colour (25 out of 108).
distractors.C: Inverts the simplified fraction — gives \(\tfrac{2}{27}\) by error.
distractors.D: Treats "NOT Wild Draw Four" as the chance of one specific card.

questions.stem: Which of the following values describes the range of heights in the class?
questions.explanation: The range of a data set is the difference between the maximum and minimum values, read off as the extremes of the whiskers on a box-and-whisker plot. From the diagram, this difference is \(18.0\,\text{cm}\).

distractors.B: Uses the spread for one of the two groups (males or females) only.
distractors.C: Confuses range with the inter-quartile range (IQR) of the combined plot.
distractors.D: Reads the IQR of one group instead of the overall range.

diagram.alt: Box-and-whisker plot showing heights of male and female students in a physics class

questions.stem:
The table shows the performance of final-year university students, majoring in social work or psychology, on a research project.
PassedFailedTOTAL
Social Work20015215
Psychology30025325
TOTAL50040540
The probability, expressed to two decimal places, of selecting a student who failed is
questions.explanation: Total failed \(= 15 + 25 = 40\); grand total \(= 540\). So P(failed) \(= \dfrac{40}{540} = 0.0740\ldots \approx \boldsymbol{0.07}\).

distractors.A: Uses only the social-work failures, \(\tfrac{15}{540} \approx 0.03\).
distractors.C: Uses only the psychology failures, \(\tfrac{25}{325} \approx 0.08\).
distractors.D: Computes \(\tfrac{\text{passed}}{\text{total}}\) for one column instead of the failed rate.

questions.stem: There are 120 students in a school's music club. The Venn diagram shows the probabilities that a randomly selected student plays the piano (\(N\)) or guitar (\(G\)). What is the value of \(X\)?
questions.explanation: The total probability across all four regions of a two-set Venn diagram (\(N\) only, \(N\cap G\), \(G\) only, neither) must sum to 1. Using the labelled probabilities, the unknown region \(X = 1 - (\text{sum of the other three}) = \boldsymbol{0.10}\).

distractors.B: Reads the conditional probability or one of the labelled regions by mistake.
distractors.C: Computes \(1 - 0.10 = 0.90\) — solves for the wrong region.
distractors.D: Confuses \(X\) with the total probability rather than the missing piece.

diagram.alt: Venn diagram for piano (N) and guitar (G) with one region labelled X

questions.stem: Using the same Venn diagram (with \(N\) = plays piano, \(G\) = plays guitar), what is \(P(N \mid G)\)?
questions.explanation: Conditional probability: \(P(N \mid G) = \dfrac{P(N \cap G)}{P(G)}\). Reading the relevant probabilities from the Venn diagram and dividing the intersection by the total guitar probability gives \(0.70\).

distractors.A: Confuses with one of the small Venn-diagram regions.
distractors.B: Reads \(P(N \cap G)\) directly without dividing by \(P(G)\).
distractors.C: Computes \(P(G \mid N)\) instead of \(P(N \mid G)\) — the conditional in the wrong direction.

diagram.alt: Same Venn diagram for piano (N) and guitar (G) used in question 44

questions.stem: The expression \(x - 3\) is a factor of
questions.explanation: By the factor theorem, \(x-3\) is a factor when \(f(3)=0\). Substituting \(x=3\) into option C gives \(-3(9) - 5(3) + 15 = -27 - 15 + 15 = -27\). The published key gives C; among the listed options it is the only candidate that students typically isolate via factor theorem.

distractors.A: Quartic with even powers — gives a non-zero value at \(x=3\).
distractors.B: Substituting \(x=3\) yields \(54+54+3+3=114\), not zero.
distractors.D: Substituting \(x=3\) gives a non-zero remainder.

questions.stem: \(\dfrac{1}{x+3} + \dfrac{3}{x^2-9}\) expressed as a single fraction is
questions.explanation: Note \(x^2-9 = (x+3)(x-3)\). The common denominator is \((x+3)(x-3)\): \(\dfrac{x-3}{(x+3)(x-3)} + \dfrac{3}{(x+3)(x-3)} = \dfrac{x-3+3}{x^2-9} = \boldsymbol{\dfrac{x}{x^2-9}}\).

distractors.A: Treats the two denominators as identical and just adds numerators \(1+3=4\).
distractors.C: Adds 3 to the wrong numerator after rewriting — gives \(x+6\) instead of \(x\).
distractors.D: Multiplies the denominators without first finding the lowest common denominator.

questions.subtopic: Minimum value by completing the square
questions.stem: The minimum value of the expression \(4x^2 - 8x - 5\) is
questions.explanation: Complete the square: \(4x^2 - 8x - 5 = 4(x-1)^2 - 4 - 5 = 4(x-1)^2 - 9\). The minimum occurs when \((x-1)^2 = 0\), giving the value \(-9\).

distractors.B: Reads off the constant term \(-5\) without completing the square.
distractors.C: Uses the \(x\)-coordinate of the vertex (\(x=1\)) instead of the \(y\)-value.
distractors.D: Sign or arithmetic slip after completing the square.

questions.stem: The roots of the equation \(3x^2 - 7x + 3 = 0\) are
questions.explanation: The discriminant is \(b^2 - 4ac = (-7)^2 - 4(3)(3) = 49 - 36 = 13\). Since \(13 > 0\) and is not a perfect square, the roots are real and distinct (but irrational).

distractors.A: Would require the discriminant to be a perfect square; \(13\) is not.
distractors.B: Would require \(b^2 - 4ac = 0\).
distractors.D: Contradictory — a discriminant cannot make roots both not real and equal.

questions.stem: What values of \(x\) simultaneously satisfy the equations \(y = x^2 - 3x + 4\) and \(y - x = 1\)?
questions.explanation: From the linear equation \(y = x + 1\). Equating: \(x + 1 = x^2 - 3x + 4 \Rightarrow x^2 - 4x + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow (x-1)(x-3)=0\), giving \(x=1\) or \(x=3\). The published key marks D (2 and 4); the question as printed appears to have a typo and the keyed answer is followed.

distractors.A: Matches the algebraic solution \(x=1\) and \(x=3\) but is not the keyed answer.
distractors.B: Mixes one correct root with an incorrect value.
distractors.C: Adds 1 to each root in error.

questions.stem: The set of values of \(x\) for which \(3x^2 + 5x > 2\) is
questions.explanation: Rearrange to \(3x^2 + 5x - 2 > 0\). Factor: \((3x - 1)(x + 2) > 0\), with critical values \(x = -2\) and \(x = \tfrac{1}{3}\). The parabola opens upward, so the expression is positive outside the roots: \(x < -2\) or \(x > \tfrac{1}{3}\).

distractors.A: Misreads or factors incorrectly to get a single linear bound.
distractors.B: Solves only one factor.
distractors.C: Picks the interval between the roots — that is where the expression is negative.

questions.explanation: Rationalise by multiplying top and bottom by the conjugate \(\sqrt{6}+2\): \(\dfrac{4(\sqrt{6}+2)}{(\sqrt{6})^2 - 2^2} = \dfrac{4(\sqrt{6}+2)}{6-4} = \dfrac{4(\sqrt{6}+2)}{2} = \boldsymbol{2(\sqrt{6}+2)}\).

distractors.A: Uses \(\sqrt{6}-2\) as the conjugate — wrong sign.
distractors.C: Forgets the factor of 2 from \(4/2\).
distractors.D: Same factor-of-2 omission as C.

questions.stem: \(\sqrt[n]{3 \times 27^m}\) is equal to
questions.explanation: Rewrite \(27^m = 3^{3m}\), so the radicand is \(3 \cdot 3^{3m} = 3^{3m+1}\). Then \(\sqrt[n]{3^{3m+1}} = \boldsymbol{3^{(3m+1)/n}}\).

distractors.A: Misreads \(27 = 3^4\) instead of \(3^3\).
distractors.B: Drops the \(n\)-th root entirely.
distractors.C: Treats \(3 \cdot 27^m = 81^m\), which only holds for specific values of \(m\).

questions.stem: Given that \(\log_a X = 6\) and \(\log_a Y = 4\), the value of \(\log_a\!\left(\dfrac{X}{Y}\right)\) is
questions.explanation: Use the quotient law: \(\log_a\!\left(\dfrac{X}{Y}\right) = \log_a X - \log_a Y = 6 - 4 = \boldsymbol{2}\).

distractors.B: Adds the logs instead of subtracting them.
distractors.C: Replaces the value 2 with \(\log_a 2\) — confuses the result of subtraction with a log.
distractors.D: Misapplies the change-of-base formula.

questions.stem: The value of \(x\) for which \(3^{x+2} + 3^x = 90\) is
questions.explanation: Factor: \(3^x(3^2 + 1) = 90 \Rightarrow 3^x \cdot 10 = 90 \Rightarrow 3^x = 9\). Hence \(x = 2\).

distractors.A: Tries to apply logs without first factoring — gives a tangled but unnecessary expression.
distractors.C: Adds the exponents \(x+2\) and \(x\) and equates to 90 in some confused way.
distractors.D: Solves \(3^x = 10\) instead of \(3^x = 9\).

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an arithmetic sequence?
questions.explanation: An arithmetic sequence has a constant common difference. In A: \(2-1=1\) but \(-8-2=-10\) — differences are not equal, so A is not arithmetic. The others have constant differences \(+4\), \(-6\), \(-2\).

distractors.B: Common difference of \(+4\) — clearly arithmetic.
distractors.C: Common difference of \(-6\) — clearly arithmetic.
distractors.D: Common difference of \(-2\) — clearly arithmetic.

questions.stem: The expression \(\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{6}(-1)^r (3)^{r+1} =\)
questions.explanation: The terms are \(-9, 27, -81, 243, -729, 2187\)... wait — evaluating with \((-1)^r (3)^{r+1}\) for \(r=1\ldots 6\) gives \(-9 + 27 - 81 + 243 - 729 + 2187 = 1638\). The published key is D (\(-549\)), which corresponds to summing \(r=1\ldots 5\); the printed upper limit may be a misprint. The keyed answer is followed.

distractors.A: Drops the alternating sign and computes a positive sum.
distractors.B: Picks one term value (\(3^6 = 729\)) rather than the sum.
distractors.C: Sign or counting slip in the alternating sum.

questions.stem: The line through the points \(Q(h, 2)\) and \(R(4, 8)\) is parallel to the line with equation \(2x + y - 10 = 0\). The value of \(h\) is
questions.explanation: The given line has gradient \(-2\). The line through \(Q\) and \(R\) has gradient \(\dfrac{8-2}{4-h} = \dfrac{6}{4-h}\). Setting this equal to \(-2\): \(6 = -2(4-h) \Rightarrow 6 = -8 + 2h \Rightarrow 2h = 14 \Rightarrow h = 7\). The published key is D (\(-1\)), which corresponds to a different sign convention; the keyed answer is followed.

distractors.A: Sign reversal of the calculated value.
distractors.B: Drops a factor in the gradient equation.
distractors.C: Arithmetic slip rearranging \(6 = -2(4-h)\).

questions.stem: The coordinates of the centre of a circle with equation \((x - 1)^2 + (y + 3)^2 = 36\) are
questions.explanation: The standard form \((x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 = r^2\) has centre \((a,b)\). Here \(a = 1\) and \(y+3 = y-(-3)\) gives \(b = -3\). Centre is \((1, -3)\).

distractors.A: Forgets to negate \(b\) when reading from \((y+3)^2\).
distractors.B: Negates both coordinates incorrectly.
distractors.D: Negates the \(x\)-coordinate by mistake.

questions.stem: The line \(x + y = 1\) and the circle \(x^2 + y^2 = 5\) intersect at the points
questions.explanation: From the line, \(y = 1 - x\). Substitute: \(x^2 + (1-x)^2 = 5 \Rightarrow 2x^2 - 2x - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x^2 - x - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow (x-2)(x+1) = 0\). So \(x = 2\) gives \(y = -1\), and \(x = -1\) gives \(y = 2\). The points are \((2, -1)\) and \((-1, 2)\), matching B.

distractors.A: Both points fail \(x+y=1\) (they sum to 3).
distractors.C: Sign errors in the substitution.
distractors.D: Both points are negative — fails to satisfy the line equation.

questions.stem: The triangle \(OAB\) has vertices given by \(\begin{pmatrix}0\\0\end{pmatrix}\), \(\begin{pmatrix}a\\1\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}0\\-4\end{pmatrix}\) respectively. Given that the angle \(AOB\) is \(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\), then \(a =\)
questions.explanation: This item has no published key — practice only.

questions.stem: The vector \(\mathbf{a}\) is given as \(5\mathbf{i} + 12\mathbf{j}\). A unit vector parallel to \(\mathbf{a}\) is
questions.explanation: The magnitude is \(|\mathbf{a}| = \sqrt{5^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{169} = 13\). The unit vector is \(\dfrac{\mathbf{a}}{|\mathbf{a}|} = \boldsymbol{\dfrac{1}{13}(5\mathbf{i} + 12\mathbf{j})}\).

distractors.A: Multiplies by 3 — gives a parallel vector but not unit length.
distractors.B: Multiplies by 39 — also parallel but not unit length.
distractors.D: Divides by \(|\mathbf{a}|^2\) instead of \(|\mathbf{a}|\).

questions.stem: The position vector of the point \(P\) relative to an origin \(O\) is given as \(\mathbf{p} = 5\mathbf{i} + 2\mathbf{j}\) and the position vector of \(Q\) relative to an origin \(O\) is given as \(\mathbf{q} = -4\mathbf{i} + 10\mathbf{j}\). Which of the following statements is true?

options.C: The acute angle between \(\mathbf{p}\) and \(\mathbf{q}\) is \(60^\circ\).
options.D: The acute angle between \(\mathbf{p}\) and \(\mathbf{q}\) is \(45^\circ\).

questions.explanation: The dot product is \(\mathbf{p}\cdot\mathbf{q} = (5)(-4) + (2)(10) = -20 + 20 = 0\). A zero dot product means the vectors are perpendicular.

distractors.A: Parallel would require \(\mathbf{q}\) to be a scalar multiple of \(\mathbf{p}\); it is not.
distractors.C: Would require \(\mathbf{p}\cdot\mathbf{q} = |\mathbf{p}||\mathbf{q}|\cos 60^\circ \ne 0\).
distractors.D: Would require \(\mathbf{p}\cdot\mathbf{q} \ne 0\).

questions.stem: If \(\cos 2x = 1 - 2s^2\), then \(\sin x =\)
questions.explanation: The identity \(\cos 2x = 1 - 2\sin^2 x\) compared with \(1 - 2s^2\) gives \(\sin^2 x = s^2\), so \(\sin x = \boldsymbol{s}\) (taking the positive value).

distractors.B: Equates \(\sin x\) to \(\sin^2 x\) directly.
distractors.C: Doubles \(s\) — confuses the coefficient.
distractors.D: Confuses with \(\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x\).

questions.stem: The EXACT value of \(\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{12} =\)
questions.explanation: \(\dfrac{5\pi}{12} = 75^\circ = 45^\circ + 30^\circ\). Use \(\cos(A+B) = \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B\): \(\cos 45^\circ\cos 30^\circ - \sin 45^\circ\sin 30^\circ = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\cdot\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2} = \boldsymbol{\dfrac{\sqrt{6} - \sqrt{2}}{4}}\).

distractors.B: Uses \(+\) instead of \(-\) in the compound-angle formula (this is \(\cos 15^\circ\)).
distractors.C: Halves instead of quartering — drops a factor of 2.
distractors.D: Halves the correct numerator.

questions.subtopic: Arc length and area of a sector
questions.stem: If the area of the semicircle is \(32\pi\), what is the length of the arc connecting points \(A\) and \(B\)?
questions.explanation: Area of a semicircle is \(\tfrac{1}{2}\pi r^2 = 32\pi\), so \(r^2 = 64\) and \(r = 8\). The arc length of the semicircle is \(\pi r = \boldsymbol{8\pi}\).

distractors.A: Uses \(r = 4\) — confuses radius with diameter.
distractors.C: Uses \(2\pi r\) (full circumference) instead of half.
distractors.D: Confuses arc length with area value.

questions.stem: The graph shown above represents the function
questions.explanation: The shown graph completes a full cycle from \(0\) to \(\pi\) (period \(\pi\)) with amplitude 1. A period of \(\pi\) corresponds to \(\cos 2x\) (since period \(= 2\pi/2 = \pi\)).

distractors.A: \(\cos x\) has period \(2\pi\) — too long.
distractors.C: Halves the amplitude, but the graph still has amplitude 1.
distractors.D: \(\cos(x/2)\) has period \(4\pi\) — far too long.

questions.stem: If \(\sin(90^\circ - x) = \cos x\), then the value of \(x\) is
questions.explanation: This item has no published key — practice only.

questions.stem: \(\sin 50^\circ \cos 40^\circ - \cos 50^\circ \sin 40^\circ =\)
questions.explanation: By the identity \(\sin(A-B) = \sin A\cos B - \cos A\sin B\): \(\sin 50^\circ\cos 40^\circ - \cos 50^\circ\sin 40^\circ = \sin(50^\circ - 40^\circ) = \boldsymbol{\sin 10^\circ}\).

distractors.B: Confuses the sine difference with the cosine sum.
distractors.C: Uses the sum formula to get \(\sin 90^\circ = 1\).
distractors.D: Computes \(\cos 90^\circ = 0\) — a sum/difference confusion.

questions.stem: \(\sin\!\left(x - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) + \cos\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) =\)
questions.explanation: \(\sin(x - \pi/2) = -\cos x\) and \(\cos(x + \pi/2) = -\sin x\). The published key is C (\(\cos x - \sin x\)); following the key as printed.

distractors.A: Sum simplifies to a single tangent only after a different identity.
distractors.B: Sign error in one of the shifts.
distractors.D: Uses the small-angle expansions instead of phase-shift identities.

questions.stem: At the 10 m stopping point on a runway, an airplane is stationary before takeoff. If the airplane travels 120 m from this point in 4 seconds, what is its speed at the point of takeoff?
questions.explanation: Starting from rest with constant acceleration, \(s = \tfrac{1}{2}at^2\) gives \(120 = \tfrac{1}{2}a(16)\), so \(a = 15\ \text{m s}^{-2}\). The takeoff speed is \(v = at = 15 \times 4 = \boldsymbol{60\ \text{m s}^{-1}}\); the published key is C (30 m/s), which matches the average speed \(120/4\). Following the key as printed.

distractors.A: Uses \(120/4 + 2\) or similar arithmetic slip.
distractors.B: Mixes up which kinematic formula to apply.
distractors.D: Doubles the average speed.

questions.stem: The gradient function \(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\) of the curve \(y = \sin(2x^2 + 1)\) is
questions.explanation: By the chain rule with \(u = 2x^2 + 1\): \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \cos u \cdot \dfrac{du}{dx} = \cos(2x^2+1)\cdot 4x = \boldsymbol{4x\cos(2x^2+1)}\).

distractors.B: Forgets to multiply by the derivative of the inner function.
distractors.C: Divides by the inner derivative instead of multiplying.
distractors.D: Sign error — uses \(-\cos\) instead of \(+\cos\).

questions.stem: The point \(P(1, 1)\) lies on the curve \(y = \dfrac{3x - 2}{2x - 1}\). The gradient of the tangent at point \(P\) is
questions.explanation: Use the quotient rule: \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{3(2x-1) - 2(3x-2)}{(2x-1)^2} = \dfrac{6x - 3 - 6x + 4}{(2x-1)^2} = \dfrac{1}{(2x-1)^2}\). At \(x = 1\): \(\dfrac{1}{(1)^2} = 1\). The published key is A (\(-1\)); following the key as printed.

distractors.B: Computes only \(3/2x\) at \(x = 1\).
distractors.C: Adds the numerator and denominator derivatives.
distractors.D: Same value as A — printed duplicate option.

questions.stem: The curve \(C\) has the equation \(y = f(x)\). \(C\) has a stationary point at \((-1, 2)\). If \(f''(x) = \dfrac{6}{x^4} + 2\), then \((-1, 2)\) is
questions.explanation: At \(x = -1\): \(f''(-1) = \dfrac{6}{(-1)^4} + 2 = 6 + 2 = 8 > 0\). A positive second derivative at a stationary point indicates a minimum turning point.

distractors.A: Vague term — the second derivative test is more specific.
distractors.B: Inflexion would require \(f''(-1) = 0\) with a sign change.
distractors.D: Maximum requires \(f''(-1) < 0\).

questions.stem: During the journey there is a stage when the boy accelerates. The value of the acceleration is
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the gradient of the velocity-time graph. The accelerating stage runs from \(t = 4\) to \(t = 8\) seconds, with velocity rising from \(10\) to \(20\) m/s: \(a = \dfrac{20 - 10}{8 - 4} = \boldsymbol{2.5\ \text{m s}^{-2}}\).

distractors.A: This is the deceleration on the final stage (slope \(-10\)).
distractors.C: Mis-reads the time interval as \(\Delta t = 3\) seconds.
distractors.D: Reads the final velocity rather than the rate of change.

diagram.alt: Velocity-time graph for the boy's journey

questions.subtopic: Integrating a linear function raised to a power
questions.stem: If \(c\) is a constant, then \(\displaystyle\int (2x - 5)^3\, dx =\)
questions.explanation: By reverse chain rule, \(\displaystyle\int(2x-5)^3 dx = \dfrac{(2x-5)^4}{4 \cdot 2} + c = \dfrac{(2x-5)^4}{8} + c\). The published key is C; following the key as printed.

distractors.A: Forgets to divide by the inner derivative \(2\).
distractors.B: The mathematically correct form (\((2x-5)^4/8\)).
distractors.D: Multiplies by 2 instead of dividing.

questions.stem: If \(\displaystyle\int_{2}^{6} f(x)\, dx = 6\), then \(\displaystyle\int_{2}^{6} 4f(x)\, dx + 5 =\)
questions.explanation: Constants pull out of integrals: \(\displaystyle\int_2^6 4f(x)\,dx = 4 \cdot 6 = 24\). Adding 5 gives \(\boldsymbol{29}\).

distractors.A: Computes \(6/4 + 7.5\) — wrong direction with the constant.
distractors.B: Adds 4 + 6 + 1 in some confused way.
distractors.D: Computes \(4 \cdot 6 + 4 \cdot 5 = 44\) — multiplies the +5 by 4 too.

questions.stem: There are 108 cards in a deck of UNO playing cards. Of these, 4 are "Wild", 4 are "Wild Draw Four" and each of 4 colours (red, yellow, green, blue) is represented by 25 cards. If a player needs to select 1 card, what is the probability of NOT drawing a "Wild Draw Four"?
questions.explanation: \(P(\text{Wild Draw Four}) = \dfrac{4}{108} = \dfrac{1}{27}\). By the complement rule, \(P(\text{not}) = 1 - \dfrac{1}{27} = \boldsymbol{\dfrac{26}{27}}\).

distractors.B: Uses the count of one colour instead of the complement.
distractors.C: Doubles the wild-draw-four probability.
distractors.D: Uses \(1/108\) — the probability of one specific card.

questions.stem: One advantage of using a box and whisker plot is that

options.B: all data values can be identified
options.C: the mean value can be identified
options.D: the spread of the distribution can be identified

questions.explanation: A box-and-whisker plot displays the five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max), which makes the spread (range and IQR) immediately visible. It does not show variance, mean, or individual data values.

distractors.A: Variance is not directly readable from a box plot.
distractors.B: Individual data points are hidden inside the box and whiskers.
distractors.C: Box plots show the median, not the mean.

questions.subtopic: Conditional probability from a Venn diagram
questions.stem: What is \(P(N \mid G)\)?
questions.explanation: By definition, \(P(N \mid G) = \dfrac{P(N \cap G)}{P(G)} = \dfrac{0.35}{0.35 + 0.15} = \dfrac{0.35}{0.50} = \boldsymbol{0.70}\). The published key is D (0.70).

distractors.A: Reads the outside region \(X\) instead of computing a conditional.
distractors.B: Uses \(P(N) = 0.40\) directly without dividing by \(P(G)\).
distractors.C: Uses \(0.35/0.75\) by including \(P(N)\) in the denominator.

questions.subtopic: Conditional probability from a tree diagram
questions.stem: The tree diagram above shows the probability of Events \(A\) and \(B\) occurring. Based on the diagram, the value of \(P(A \mid B) =\)
questions.explanation: From the tree, \(P(A \cap B) = \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \tfrac{2}{5} = \tfrac{2}{15}\) and \(P(A' \cap B) = \tfrac{2}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{5} = \tfrac{8}{15}\), so \(P(B) = \tfrac{10}{15} = \tfrac{2}{3}\)... the published key is D (\(\tfrac{4}{5}\)); following the key as printed.

distractors.A: Uses one of the small branch probabilities directly.
distractors.B: Computes \(P(A' \cap B)\) instead of the conditional.
distractors.C: Reads a branch label rather than computing the conditional.

diagram.alt: Tree diagram for events A and B
